There is no reporting responsibility under the Polit 
Reform Act with respect to rides to and from work which you 
receive from a lobbyist. 
According to the information you 
have provided me, you are not covered by a Conflict of Interest 
Code, and thus have no reporting responsibilities of any 
kind. 
Since your duties in the mail room consist only of 
sorting mail and framing bills, you are neither a legislat 
official nor engaged in legislative action within the mean 
of the Act so as to create any reporting responsibilities in 
a lobbyist.

Chief, Legal Division 


December 18, 1979 
Thomas M. Sobel 
Chief, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street Building 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Sobel: 
Pursuant to our previous conversation relating to your 
letter dated September 3 to Nelson Whitely, do I understand 
correctly that I have no lobbyist reporting responsibilities 
arising from the fact that I give Mr. Whitely a ride to and 
from work daily? 
Thanks very much for your counsel. 
A confirming response 
would be greatly appreciated. 
Very truly yours, 
Robert P. Houston

sections.conclusion: There is no reporting responsibility under the Political Reform Act with respect to rides to and from work which you receive from a lobbyist. According to the information you have provided me, you are not covered by a Conflict of Interest Code, and thus have no reporting responsibilities of any kind. Since your duties in the mail room consist only of sorting mail and framing bills, you are neither a legislative official nor engaged in legislative action within the meaning of the Act so as to create any reporting responsibilities in a lobbyist.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a lobbyist have reporting responsibilities under the Political Reform Act for providing rides to work to a Senate mail room employee?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A lobbyist has no reporting responsibilities for providing rides to work to a Senate mail room employee because the employee is not a legislative official and is not engaged in legislative action under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Two separate documents presented together - initial FPPC advice letter followed by confirming inquiry from affected lobbyist. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers; conclusion synthesized from body text. Document quality is poor with OCR artifacts affecting readability (e.g., 'te of CaiiforILia', 'legislat'). Letter references prior correspondence dated September 3 (which is not included in this extraction).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a lobbyist have reporting responsibilities under the Political Reform Act for providing rides to work to a Senate mail room employee?

CONCLUSION: There is no reporting responsibility under the Political Reform Act with respect to rides to and from work which you receive from a lobbyist. According to the information you have provided me, you are not covered by a Conflict of Interest Code, and thus have no reporting responsibilities of any kind. Since your duties in the mail room consist only of sorting mail and framing bills, you are neither a legislative official nor engaged in legislative action within the meaning of the Act so as to create any reporting responsibilities in a lobbyist.

embedding.summary: This document contains an FPPC advice letter dated December 3, 1979 from Thomas M. Sobel to Nelson Whitely regarding reporting responsibilities for rides provided by a lobbyist, followed by a confirming inquiry from the lobbyist Robert P. Houston dated December 18, 1979.
